A 2 kg trolley moving at 3 m/s collides and sticks to a stationary 1 kg trolley. Using conservation of momentum, calculate their combined velocity after the collision.
What safety feature in cars is designed to increase the time over which a collision occurs, reducing force on passengers?
State the equation linking force, change in momentum and time (used to explain safety features).
acceptedAnswers: force equals change in momentum over time

questions.prompt: In a transverse wave, the oscillations are:
options: Parallel to the direction of energy transfer
Perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer

questions.prompt: In a longitudinal wave, the oscillations are:
options: Perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer
Parallel to the direction of energy transfer
Always at right angles to the wave

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of a longitudinal wave?
Which of these is an example of a transverse wave?
Waves transfer energy without transferring matter.
A longitudinal wave has areas of compression and rarefaction.
All waves require a medium (material) to travel through, including light.
Name one example of a transverse wave.
Name one example of a longitudinal wave.
What do all waves transfer, without transferring the medium itself?
The equation linking wave speed, frequency and wavelength is:
What is the unit of frequency?
A wave has a frequency of 5 Hz and wavelength of 2 m. What is its speed?
Amplitude of a wave is:
options: The distance between two adjacent peaks
The maximum displacement from the undisturbed (rest) position
The number of waves per second

questions.prompt: Wavelength is the distance from one point on a wave to the identical point on the next wave (e.g. peak to peak).
Frequency is the number of waves passing a point per second.
Increasing amplitude always increases wave frequency.
A wave travels at 340 m/s with a wavelength of 2 m. Calculate its frequency.
What is the unit of wavelength?
Describe the required practical method for measuring the frequency, wavelength and speed of waves in a ripple tank.
acceptedAnswers: measure wavelength with a ruler, count waves per second for frequency, then use v=f*wavelength
use a strobe/ruler to measure wavelength and count oscillations for frequency

questions.prompt: When a wave is reflected off a surface, the angle of incidence is:
options: Always greater than the angle of reflection
Always less than the angle of reflection
Equal to the angle of reflection

questions.prompt: Refraction of a wave occurs when it:
options: Changes speed and direction as it crosses a boundary between different media

questions.prompt: When light travels from air into a denser medium like glass, it:
options: Speeds up and bends away from the normal
Slows down and bends towards the normal
Stays at the same speed and direction

questions.prompt: The normal, used when describing reflection and refraction, is:
options: A line parallel to the surface
An imaginary line perpendicular (at 90°) to the surface at the point of incidence
The path of the incident ray
The path of the refracted ray only

questions.prompt: Sound waves can be reflected, producing echoes.
A wave's frequency changes when it is refracted from one medium to another.
Waves can be absorbed, transmitted, or reflected when they meet a boundary between two different materials.
State the law of reflection relating angle of incidence and angle of reflection.
acceptedAnswers: angle of incidence equals angle of reflection
angle of incidence = angle of reflection

questions.prompt: What happens to the direction of a wave's travel when it undergoes refraction (excluding travelling along the normal)?
Name the imaginary line drawn perpendicular to a surface, used as a reference for measuring angles of incidence and reflection.
All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed through a:
Which type of electromagnetic wave has the longest wavelength?
Which type of electromagnetic wave has the highest frequency?
Electromagnetic waves are:
options: Longitudinal waves that require a medium
Transverse waves that can travel through a vacuum
Only able to travel through solids

questions.prompt: As frequency increases across the electromagnetic spectrum, wavelength decreases.
Higher frequency electromagnetic waves, like gamma and X-rays, generally carry more energy and can be more hazardous.
Visible light has a higher frequency than gamma rays.
List the seven types of electromagnetic wave in order of increasing frequency, starting with radio waves.
acceptedAnswers: radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, x-rays, gamma rays
radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, x-ray, gamma

questions.prompt: Name the part of the electromagnetic spectrum that our eyes can detect.
What do all electromagnetic waves transfer from a source to an absorber?

quizzes.title: 6.5 Uses and Applications of Electromagnetic Waves

questions.prompt: Which type of electromagnetic wave is commonly used for satellite communication and cooking food?
Which type of electromagnetic wave is used for remote controls and night vision?
Which type of electromagnetic wave is used to kill microbes and sterilise medical equipment?
X-rays are commonly used in medicine to:
options: Treat broken bones by heating them
Image bones and internal structures of the body

questions.prompt: Ultraviolet radiation is used in sunbeds and can also cause skin damage/cancer with overexposure.
Radio waves are used for broadcasting television and radio programmes.
Gamma rays are used for broadcasting radio signals over long distances.
Name one everyday use of infrared radiation.
Name one medical use of gamma radiation.
Give one hazard associated with excessive exposure to ultraviolet radiation.
A converging (convex) lens causes parallel rays of light to:
A diverging (concave) lens causes parallel rays of light to:
options: Meet at a single focal point
Spread out, appearing to come from a focal point

questions.prompt: The distance from the centre of a lens to its principal focus is called the:
A real image formed by a lens can be:
options: Only viewed directly by the eye, never on a screen
Captured on a screen because light rays actually meet there
Always the same size as the object

questions.prompt: A virtual image cannot be projected onto a screen because light rays only appear to come from it.
A convex lens is also known as a converging lens.
A concave lens causes light rays to converge to a real focal point.
Name the type of lens that causes parallel light rays to converge to a point.
Name the type of lens that causes parallel light rays to diverge.
What is the magnification equation, linking image height and object height?
acceptedAnswers: magnification equals image height divided by object height

quizzes.title: 6.7 Visible Light, Colour and Black Body Radiation (Physics Only)

questions.prompt: An opaque object appears a certain colour because it:
options: Reflects only the wavelength(s) corresponding to that colour and absorbs others
Emits that colour of light itself

questions.prompt: A perfect black body is an object that:
options: Reflects all radiation incident upon it
Absorbs all wavelengths of electromagnetic radiation incident upon it, with no reflection or transmission

questions.prompt: As the temperature of a black body increases, the intensity of radiation emitted at all wavelengths:
options: Increases, and the peak wavelength shifts to shorter wavelengths

questions.prompt: A white object appears white in white light because it:
options: Reflects all colours of light roughly equally

questions.prompt: A good absorber of radiation is also a good emitter of radiation, at a given temperature.
All objects, including the Earth, emit and absorb electromagnetic radiation.
A black object is a poor absorber but an excellent reflector of light.
What colour does a filter transmit if red light is shone through a red filter?
Name the model used in physics to describe a perfect absorber and emitter of radiation.
What happens to the peak wavelength of radiation emitted by a black body as its temperature increases?

quizzes.title: 7.1 Permanent and Induced Magnets and Magnetic Fields

questions.prompt: A permanent magnet:
options: Only produces a magnetic field when placed in another magnetic field
Produces its own magnetic field at all times
Loses its magnetism instantly when moved

questions.prompt: An induced magnet is a material that:
options: Is always magnetic, even far from other magnets
Becomes magnetic only when placed in a magnetic field

questions.prompt: Two like magnetic poles (e.g. north-north) will:
options: Have no effect on each other

questions.prompt: The magnetic field around a bar magnet is strongest:
options: At the centre of the magnet
At the poles of the magnet
Only outside the magnet's field lines

questions.prompt: Magnetic field lines around a bar magnet point from north to south outside the magnet.
An induced magnet always attracts a permanent magnet, regardless of which pole is brought close.
A compass needle aligns with the Earth's magnetic field, allowing it to indicate direction.
Name two materials commonly used to make permanent or induced magnets.
What happens to an induced magnet's magnetism when it is removed from the magnetic field?
Name the instrument that uses the Earth's magnetic field to indicate direction.
The motor effect occurs when:
options: A current-carrying wire is placed in a magnetic field, experiencing a force
Two magnets are placed far apart
A wire is heated in a vacuum
A magnet is dropped onto a table

questions.prompt: The equation linking force, magnetic flux density, current and length of conductor is:
What rule can be used to predict the direction of force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field?
An electric motor uses the motor effect to convert:
The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is greatest when the conductor is perpendicular to the field.
Reversing the current direction or the magnetic field direction reverses the direction of the force in the motor effect.
The motor effect only occurs when a wire carries no current at all.

Name the rule used to find the direction of the force, current, and magnetic field in the motor effect.

quizzes.title: 7.3 Induced Potential, Transformers and the National Grid (Physics Only)

questions.prompt: Electromagnetic induction occurs when:
options: A magnet is moved relative to a coil of wire, or vice versa, inducing a potential difference
Two magnets are held stationary next to each other
A wire is placed far from any magnetic field
A current flows through a resistor only

questions.prompt: A step-up transformer has more turns on which coil?
The transformer equation relates potential difference and number of turns as:
options: Vp x Vs = Np x Ns

questions.prompt: Transformers only work with which type of current?
options: Both dc and ac equally well

questions.prompt: A generator uses electromagnetic induction (moving a coil in a magnetic field) to produce an alternating potential difference.
An ideal transformer transfers all electrical power from primary to secondary coil with no loss.
A step-down transformer increases the voltage from primary to secondary coil.
A transformer has 100 turns on the primary coil and 400 on the secondary coil, with 10 V input. Calculate the output voltage (assume ideal transformer).
Name the device that uses electromagnetic induction to generate electricity by rotating a coil in a magnetic field.
Why are step-up transformers used before electricity is transmitted across the National Grid?
acceptedAnswers: to increase voltage and reduce current, reducing energy loss in cables
to reduce power loss during transmission

quizzes.title: 8.1 The Solar System and Orbital Motion

questions.prompt: Our Solar System consists of the Sun and objects that orbit it, including:
options: Planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids and comets

questions.prompt: A moon is a natural satellite that orbits:
options: The centre of the galaxy only
Nothing; it moves in a straight line

questions.prompt: Stars form from clouds of dust and gas pulled together by:

After its main sequence phase, a star much more massive than the Sun can eventually explode as a supernova.
Our Sun is expected to eventually become a red giant, then a white dwarf.
Every star in the universe will eventually become a black hole.
What force keeps planets in orbit around the Sun?

options: A force in the direction of motion, speeding it up
A centripetal force directed towards the centre of the orbit
A force pushing it away from the orbited body

questions.prompt: In a stable orbit, if the orbital radius is greater, the orbital speed needed is:
A satellite in a stable orbit has a velocity that is constantly changing in:

A change in speed or direction of an orbiting object requires a resultant (centripetal) force.
Satellites in orbit have a constant velocity because their speed and direction never change.
Geostationary satellites orbit the Earth once every 24 hours, staying above the same point on Earth's surface.
What provides the centripetal force that keeps a satellite in circular orbit around a planet?
Name one practical use of artificial satellites.
Why does an orbiting satellite maintain a constant speed even though a resultant force acts on it?
acceptedAnswers: the force acts perpendicular to its velocity, changing direction not speed
the centripetal force only changes direction, not speed

questions.prompt: Red-shift refers to the observation that light from distant galaxies is shifted towards:
Red-shift provides evidence that:
options: Distant galaxies are moving away from us and the universe is expanding
Stars are moving closer to Earth
Light travels slower over long distances

questions.prompt: Generally, the further away a galaxy is, the greater its observed red-shift, meaning it is moving away:
options: At exactly the same speed as nearby galaxies

questions.prompt: The evidence from red-shift supports which scientific theory about the origin of the universe?
Red-shift is similar to the Doppler effect observed with sound waves from a moving source.
Cosmic microwave background radiation is considered further evidence supporting the Big Bang theory.
Red-shift evidence suggests that all galaxies are moving closer together over time.
